posts.body_text:
Fully agree.
SaaS will just absorb AI by adding agentic layers so users can access features via prompts instead of complex workflows.
posts.stimulus:
"SaaS is dead".
I don't believe it.

January isn’t even over, and the feed is already full of confident predictions:

 SaaS is dying
 AI agents will replace platforms
 Web coding + no-code = the end of software

I skipped public year-end summaries this year. But this narrative is too loud to ignore.

SaaS isn’t disappearing.
It’s mutating.

Yes, AI agents change how we interact with products. Yes, building tools is becoming easier.

But the idea that businesses will suddenly build and run their own infrastructure - even with AI - is detached from reality.

Because infrastructure is still:
1⃣ expensive
2⃣ complex
3⃣ risky to scale

And owning it is rarely a competitive advantage. Most companies don’t want more things to build. They want systems that just work, integrate fast, and adapt to their workflows.

The future looks like this:

– SaaS becomes AI-native
– Products turn into intelligent layers, not rigid platforms
– AI agents don’t replace SaaS - they operate inside it

So no - SaaS isn’t dying. Bad, inflexible SaaS is.

 What do you think:

Will AI replace SaaS - or will SaaS quietly absorb it and evolve?

posts.body_text:
I’m doubling down on checker skills, catching bugs, missing constraints, and edge cases AI introduces due to incomplete prompts.
 AI can write code; engineers must protect outcomes.
posts.stimulus:
AI stopped being a cute copilot

1/ Top engineers report AI agents now write most of their production code, across web, backend, and even C.
2/ New models (Opus 4.5, GPT‑5.2, Gemini 3) crossed an invisible line: suddenly, complex tasks that used to be painful are now “one‑shot” solvable.

 The bad news for “just coders”

1/ Being “the person who types fast in language X” is rapidly losing value; AI can jump stacks better than most humans.
2/ Tasks like prototyping, refactoring, and implementing well‑defined tickets will be increasingly delegated to AI.

 The good news for engineers

1/ Clarifying what to build: breaking down vague ideas into precise behavior, edge cases, and constraints.
2/ Owning quality: testing, CI/CD, observability, reliability, performance, security, tech debt.
3/ Thinking like a product engineer: close to users, shipping fast, deciding what matters vs noise.

AI writes more lines; humans own the outcomes.

 The ugly side

1/ More code → more bugs, more complexity, more infra cost; change failure rates are already going up in some orgs.
2/ Mobile AI coding means “coding from your phone” becomes normal, and work–life boundaries risk being erased even more.

 What this means for careers

1/ Juniors will be pushed to think like seniors earlier: architecture, testing, trade‑offs, not just syntax.
2/ A solid CS / software engineering foundation may become a minimum bar, not a nice‑to‑have.
3/ Demand for good software engineers might actually rise, as the amount of AI‑generated software explodes.

Are you already letting AI write 50–90% of your code?
What skills are you actively doubling down on for the next 5 years?
#AI #SoftwareEngineering #DeveloperExperience #LLM #ProductEngineering

posts.body_text: Same realization here. The hype said agents would replace teams, but in reality AI works best when it supports people.
posts.stimulus:
"AI agents were supposed to take over in 2025. Instead, 42% of companies just abandoned theirs."

Since November, companies that rushed to deploy AI agents are quietly pulling back. Projects are stalling. Budgets are being questioned. And hardly anyone's talking about it publicly.

While tech giants were celebrating their agent launches in November -OpenAI's Agent Mode, Perplexity's Comet Browser, Google's Gemini 3 - the reality in production environments tells a different story. Deloitte's Tech Trends 2025 report reveals that only 11% of organizations currently use AI agents in production, despite intense hype.

Here's what's actually breaking them:

Agentic AI systems already support scientific discovery, software development, and clinical research, yet they still struggle with unreliable tool use, weak long-horizon planning, and poor generalization. A Stanford and Harvard research paper published in December finally explains why agents feel impressive in demos, then completely fall apart in real use.

The uncomfortable data:

In November, a study found that agents powered by top LLMs from OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Anthropic failed to complete many straightforward workplace tasks by themselves. But when the same agents worked alongside humans who knew what they were doing, success rates shot up dramatically.

Sam Altman announced a 'code red' for ChatGPT in December, asking his teams to refocus away from agents and get back to improving the core ChatGPT user experience. That tells you everything about where we actually are versus where the hype said we'd be.

What's actually working:

The pattern is clear - agents aren't replacing humans, they're augmenting experts. The organizations succeeding aren't deploying autonomous agents; they're building human-AI collaboration systems where judgment-heavy tasks stay with people.

posts.stimulus:
We’re seeing this a lot in the automation space, and honestly, I’ve been guilty of it too.

Most automation agencies make one mistake, they try to sell automation.
But selling automation often adds cost before it adds value.

Because in the early stages, most of what automation promises can actually be done manually, faster, cheaper, and with more context.

The real need for automation begins when manual work becomes the bottleneck, when scaling becomes painful, repetitive, and error-prone.

That’s when automation stops being a “nice-to-have” and becomes an enabler of growth.

So instead of trying to sell automation, we build it where the pain is real, where teams are already doing the work, and scaling it manually no longer makes sense.

At CodingRippler, that’s how we think about automation,
not as a product to push, but as a capability that emerges naturally when scaling requires it.

Maybe automation shouldn’t be sold, it should be discovered.

posts.body_text: AI isn’t about disappearing people, it’s about making them superstars at what they do best!
posts.stimulus:
Want to see an AI magic trick?

Many big companies are rushing to perfect the 'make employees disappear' trick. But I think they're completely missing the mark.

When Voice AI can handle half of all frontline banking interactions, leaders face a fascinating choice: reduce their workforce, or redirect that freed-up human potential into deeper customer relationships and revenue growth.

Let's fast forward to 2035. Which approach builds a more loyal customer community?

I get it—when banking leaders first explore AI, immediate cost reduction is appealing. It’s a bit of an ‘abracadabra’ moment. With today's pressures, who wouldn't consider it? 

But when we dig into their core strategic goals—sustainable growth, market resilience, competitive edge—the conversation shifts. They begin to see AI not just as a replacement strategy, but as a way to recalibrate their teams for growth AND cost savings.

The organizations that truly get it? They're using AI to achieve what was previously impossible with their frontline teams. That's where the real magic happens. No tricks, no illusions—just the transformative power of human potential amplified by AI.

Apologies in advance to the magic community if my use of abracadabra isn’t appropriate in this context.

posts.body_text: AI is a great tool, but if we stop thinking ourselves, it will end up replacing us
posts.stimulus:
I have started asking candidates, "Can you do this without AI?"
Seems like a strange thing to ask, since we are in the business of AI.
 
But what I have been observing, with alarm, is how for some folks, AI has replaced critical thinking and effort.
 
There's good research to back this. MIT found that people using ChatGPT showed 68% weaker neural connectivity. Their brains simply became less active.

That's where the biggest risk and opportunity with AI lies. 

If you want to offload thinking, it helps you become lazier.
If you want to think sharper, it becomes the best teacher. 
 
With each interaction, you make that choice. 
To become better or to become more dependent. 
 
And that is the biggest irony. 
AI will replace those who purely depend on AI.
 
#AI #CriticalThinking #FutureOfWork

posts.body_text: Jonathan Coulter Exactly! Search today is about being findable across AI, agents, and humans. That’s why we focus on clear, factual, and crawlable content that shows up wherever your audience asks
posts.stimulus:
SEO isn’t just Google anymore, it’s “can assistants and agents find you?”

With a new website I'm digging into SEO and reminded just how long Google can take for visibility. 

However, in reality, a lot of buyers now ask ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini before they turn to Google and more teams are deploying AI agents (support, scheduling, sales ops) that need the same raw material to answer well.

Assistants and agent optimisation is now as important as SEO

• Make it findable, not fancy. Plain, public pages that answer real questions. FAQs, pricing ranges, locations, “who we’re for/not for.”

• Be quotable. Short, clear proof pages. Assistants love a clean source.

• Keep facts consistent everywhere. Site, LinkedIn, directories. Contradictions make assistants less confident in their recommendation.

• Write like the prompt. Use content the way people ask: “Best [service] for [problem] in [location]?” Then answer it straight.

• Document the process. Steps, SLAs, process stick when models summarise or agents explain decisions.

• Little and often. Fresh, factual updates compound faster than one perfect essay you never publish.

Does this surface faster than classic SEO?

Often yes for assistants and agents. Clear, crawlable, consistent content can be picked up in hours or days, meaningful Google movement is usually weeks or months. (Crawlability still matters for both)

Curious, has anyone had leads say, “we asked ChatGPT/Claude and your name came up” What did they see?

posts.body_text:
Great post Panagiotis Spiliotopoulos! AI in pharma can really help make work faster and more accurate. I like your idea about AI agents working together like a team, it feels like the future!

At CodingRippler, we build AI tools like Sales Rep Assistants and smart automations. Would love to connect and see how we can work together on your AI projects
posts.stimulus:
So lets talk about AI in pharma. 
Generative AI can be something that can improve speed and accuracy of information. Access to the necessary data is vital of course. 
E.g. a Sales Rep Assistant that can answer by retrieving the information requested thus giving a better accuracy, either for preparation, follow-up, goal achievement, etc. 
Agentic AI can be something that allows to plan and execute tasks. And of course I have seen agents assisting in creating records, while dictating or planning interactions to achieve targets, but my take is undertaking more complex tasks, like plan an event or a product launch or an informative/marketing campaign. All the latter have a more complex project approach. 
Of course, in all scenarios tools and access to data is key. An LLM is not just enough or a monolithic approach to an AI implementation, e.g. a GenAI without RAG technique (Retrieval-Augmented Generation). 
I would say that the IT eco-system has to prioritize communication between the systems, not just access but planning for an AI agent to AI agent communication. 
The future? Probably a human asking an AI agent to help with a complex task and the AI agent asks other AI agents for help. (I do not know why but I had the "Avengers Assemble" scene pop in my mind)

Final note. Humans must be in the equation to validate and have the ownership of the task/outcome. And do not be afraid to have your AI either reply “I do not know” or “I cannot assist, need clarification, do not have access, etc”. Alternative, answers and results that are not the correct.

posts.body_text: So true Kyri Chrysandreas! Too much data can create confusion instead of clarity. At CodingRippler, we help businesses focus on the right metrics by building smart dashboards and automation, so leaders can make quick, confident decisions without drowning in numbers
posts.stimulus:
More data doesn’t mean better decisions.

I worked with a leadership team that had no shortage of dashboards. Every metric imaginable was available - uptime, throughput, cost per transaction, customer clicks, NPS, you name it. They had so much data that meetings turned into endless debates over which numbers actually mattered.

The result? Decisions slowed down. Teams second-guessed themselves. Opportunities passed by.

The common belief is that more data equals more clarity. In reality, more data often equals more noise. When everything is measured, nothing feels meaningful. Leaders don’t need all the data - they need the right data.

Better decisions come from context, not quantity.

• Focus on a small set of metrics tied to outcomes, not activity.
• Translate data into insights that the business can act on.
• Empower leaders with clarity, not clutter.

→ Drowning in dashboards doesn’t make you data-driven.
→ Making confident, timely decisions does.

#DataDriven #BetterDecisions #DataQuality #DataStrategy #BusinessIntelligence #DecisionMaking #DataAnalysis #InsightfulData #DataOverload #SmartDecisions

posts.body_text:
Totally agree! Ojassvi Pradhan,

At Coding Rippler, we’ve seen how AI isn’t just improving existing products but creating completely new opportunities. It’s exciting to help businesses build solutions that customers didn’t even know they needed and then watch those ideas turn into whole new markets!
posts.stimulus:
The most exciting AI products aren’t fighting for market share. They’re creating new markets.
When you’re solving expressed needs, you’re usually entering an existing category — trying to do it cheaper, faster, or better.
But when you’re solving latent needs, you often end up creating a category that didn’t exist before.

Think about it:
Users didn’t ask for ride-hailing apps. They asked for “more reliable autos.” The category of on-demand mobility was born.
Nobody asked for ChatGPT. They asked for “better search” or “faster writing.” A new category of AI copilots was born.

That’s the unique power of AI products.
They don’t just compete in markets — they can redefine what a market is.
For Product Managers, this means the questions shift from:
 “How do we beat competitors?”
 “How do we shape how people think about this problem in the first place?”
Because creating a category isn’t just about technology.
It’s about storytelling, education, and building trust for something users never thought they needed.
And that’s the real privilege of building in AI today:
Not just making products better — but making new possibilities real.

#AIProductManagement #CategoryCreation #LatentNeeds #ProductLeadership

posts.body_text:
Totally agree! In startups, things are always moving fast and it can feel messy. 

At CodingRippler , we help startups do just that automating workflows and building smart solutions so teams can focus on moving fast with confidence!
posts.stimulus:
One thing I’ve learned in nearly a decade of working with startups: No one is handing you a playbook (and if they do, it's typically a draft that's meant to be tweaked, tested, and rewritten as you go)

In a startup, things move fast. Priorities can change in an instant, and structures don’t yet exist. Sometimes you’re building the plane while flying it (actually, it always feels that way ).

And that means self-direction and motivation aren’t just nice to have, they’re a necessity of survival.

But here’s the truth, people don’t always talk about:
- Sometimes you feel alone.
- Sometimes you feel lost.
- Sometimes you wonder if what you’re doing is moving your company in the right direction.

That’s normal.

Self-direction doesn’t mean having all the answers. It means:
- Trusting yourself enough to keep moving forward.
- Backing your gut with data, not just opinions.
- Creating clarity when no one else has it yet.
- Taking action, even in the gray areas.

Startups can sometimes feel messy and isolating. But if you can find your footing in that ambiguity, rely on your team, and try to find confidence in the fact that everyone is in the same boat, that’s when the magic happens! Just keep moving forward, testing, retesting... If it were easy, everyone would do it!

#startups #techstartups #innovation

posts.stimulus:
Celebrating 5 Years at Microsoft 

If you had told me when I graduated from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology that I would one day be a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft with a Master of Science in Software Engineering, I might not have believed you. Yet here I am in 2025, reflecting on an incredible journey filled with growth, opportunity, and gratitude.

First, I owe so much to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program, which gave me my start in programming and changed the trajectory of my career. A heartfelt thanks also to Deloitte, where I learned invaluable soft skills and the true power of an inclusive and collaborative organization.

I’m deeply grateful to the West Virginia Army National Guard for enabling me to earn my master’s degree debt-free through their exceptional tuition assistance programs. If you’re looking for the best deal in education, this is it!

My time as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer in the Army National Guard has taught me leadership and resilience in ways that no classroom or corporate training ever could.

To all the incredible civilian and military leaders and mentors I’ve had the privilege of learning from over the years—your guidance and support have truly shaped who I am today. Your impact on my life has been profound, and I am forever grateful.

This journey is a testament to the power of hard work and having clear goals. It truly is the American dream.

I’m incredibly thankful, humbled, and blessed to be where I am today. Here’s to the next chapter!

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #ArmyNationalGuard #Gratitude #AmericanDream

Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

This isn’t just about removing the standing power throw or renaming the ACFT. It’s about raising the bar for readiness, lethality, and shared standards.

Key changes:
• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run
• Sex-neutral, age-normed standards for 21 combat MOSs
• Min score: 350 total / 60 per event (combat MOSs)
• Min score: 300 total (combat-support MOSs)

This change reinforces our commitment to excellence - regardless of rank, MOS, or gender.

We lead with standards.

Are you ready to meet the challenge?

#ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #AJOC #ArmyStrong #JuniorOfficers #CombatReady #WarriorEthos #Lethality #Readiness

posts.stimulus:
I built a solution to reduce cognitive load when working with multiple LLMs

The problem: You ask ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini the same question, then mentally synthesize three different answers.

The solution: "Merge mode" for PolyGPT

How it works:
 - Log into AI providers in split-screen (no API keys needed)
 - Ask your question once
 - All LLMs respond simultaneously
 - 4th window shows an AI-synthesized answer with citations [1] [2] [3]
 - Follow-up questions continue the merged conversation

Instead of reading three responses, you get one coherent answer combining the best of all models.

Inspired by Andrej Karpathy's "LLM council" concept, adapted for web-based LLMs.

Built on open source PolyGPT. Mac executable + code coming soon.

How do you handle comparing LLM responses?

#AI #LLM #ProductivityTools #OpenSource

posts.stimulus:
While going through the DSA fundamentals on NeetCode, I stumbled upon something really cool: when a dynamic array runs out of space, it doesn’t just add one more slot - it doubles its entire capacity!

Imagine this: instead of constantly resizing every time you add an element (which would be super slow), the array says, “Hey, I need more space!” and allocates twice the amount it currently has.

This seemingly simple idea is genius:

- Amortized O(1) insertion - most additions are super fast

- Reduced memory allocations - fewer expensive resize operations

- Predictable growth pattern - capacity grows as 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32…

It's one of those "obvious in hindsight" optimizations that make you appreciate the thought that goes into fundamental data structures. Sometimes the most elegant solutions hide in plain sight!

#DataStructures #Algorithms #SoftwareEngineering #Programming

posts.body_text: Yup. Especially in a globalized world where it’s easy for people to misinterpret ours and everyone else”s behaviors and signals.
posts.stimulus:
IIT made me feel I wasn’t that “intelligent”. With an All India Rank of 1720, I took Metallurgy and anyone who’s gone to IIT knows that Metallurgy students are seen “not as smart” as Computer Science or Electrical, or even Mechanical ones. 

To add perspective, every year 1,500,000 students appear for engineering exams in India. So at 1720, I was in top 0.001 and yet I felt stupid because I wasn’t the best in Maths and Science. 

I was a writer, an impeccable orator and an artist. I had self awareness, I could empathise with people and communicate my thoughts effectively. But who cares? Those are “soft skills” right? None of that counted as “intelligence”. 

I find it unsettling how our schooling system completely ignores core competencies like emotional intelligence and communication skills. And we grow up with this skewed definition of intelligence. It’s almost a scam. The moment you leave college and enter a profession, you realise that work is all about people (At least for the vast majority) 
You are building for people, working with people. 

Your soft skills are not nice-to-haves. They are key to professional growth. I wish at 18, someone told this to me and I paid more attention to hone my soft skills. 

What do you think? Do share your thoughts. 

#education #india #iit

posts.stimulus:
The colors of the family tree

Have you looked through old B&W pictures and wondered what the world looked like back then?

There's a certain resonance to shades of gray but once in a while it evokes curiosity.

The Singapore government worked on a project to convert old B&W images into color

They wanted to keep the texture and feel of the old pictures.

However, they're not using artists to do the job. This would not have been a must-do project

Apart from the cost and time this would entail, it was not practical to do manually.

So they used a bank of images to train the AI to work its magic on the palette

You can see the results, both the successful and the failed ones in the links below.

It worked best on high resolution pictures featuring humans and natural scenery.

Not the saturated, glossy and detailed millions of colors you see in images today.

May be that's for the best.

A colorful but slightly faded version of the world then

Not just in sepia

The project is now open for anyone to upload and experiment with their old B&W images

Are there family pictures you'd like to see in color?

posts.body_text: My recommendation is read fiction. All these motivational/inspirational books are just telling you to do this and that. You can get a good 2-page summary for each from an LLM. But reading a good fiction is like listening the author tell it to you; no LLM can tell it like the author, and removing a single sentence might change the impression. Finally, fiction is the only category where the truth is told without the need to be politically correct and buzzword-laden.
posts.stimulus:
After a couple of years of barely reading, I finally got back into the habit last year. I read around 15 books in total, and I want share my top 8 - in the order I read them. I’ve also included a few quotes I liked to give you a quick glimpse into each book.

1⃣ The Magic of Thinking Big — David J. Schwartz 9.5/10
A book recommended by a friend, Davit Abgaryan, PhD. 
A book to recalibrate your internal standards.
Quote: “Your most important product is yourself. Never sell yourself short.”

2⃣ The ONE Thing — Gary W. Keller & Jay Papasan 10/10
Possibly the most actionable book I read. The question that stayed with me all year:
Quote: “What’s the ONE thing I can do such that by doing it, everything else will be easier or unnecessary?”

3⃣ The 12 Week Year — Brian Moran & Michael Lennington 10/10
Very inspiring, packed with practical takeaways. 
Quote: "Many people spend a significant amount of time and energy attempting to eliminate their weaknesses, while it is the focusing on your strengths that produces your greatest achievements." 

4⃣ Extreme Ownership & Discipline Equals Freedom — Jocko Willink & Leif Babin 9/10 
Read them in that order to increase the effect! 
Quote: "There is no excuse for not getting out of bed, especially when all rests on that decisive moment."

5⃣ The Power of Positive Thinking — Norman Vincent Peale 10/10
The first half of the book was a bit challenging to get through, but after that, it became absolutely AMAZING. It even inspired me to start reading the Old and New Testaments.
Quote: "One of the greatest tragedies of the average person is the tendency to spend our whole lives, perfecting our faults." 

6⃣ Outlive — Peter Attia. 8.5 /10
A wake-up call for anyone who’s crossed the 30-year mark. Start prioritizing your health today — your future self will thank you!

7⃣ Areté — Brian Johnson. 9/10
Plenty of valuable insights from numerous brilliant thinkers and references, if you want to explore further.
Quote: "To thine own self be true. Trust yourself. Every heart vibrates to that iron string."

8⃣ The Miracle Morning — Hal Elrod 8.5/10
Structure your morning. Shape your life.
Quote: "Life stays the same because we stay the same."

Share your top 9/10 or 10/10 books from recent years — I’d love to see your recommendations

Xfinity (aka Comcast) has been causing so much trouble with my Internet connection. The price constantly goes up, and the quality regularly goes down. Today it was particularly terrible; I've been losing connection every 5 mins in the evening and every ~20 earlier during the day.

posts.stimulus:
Random idea for reducing waste:

Imagine going on Amazon retail website (or any other eCommerce giant's), ordering something, and in shipping details picking an option "Ship in original box" or "Do not package". This could be eligible for majority of items that are already packaged in their factory boxes. So then Amazon would just print the shipping label on the original box and send it out. Maybe I am missing something, but I think as long as the label is on a flat surface and legible, the color of the box shouldn't matter. Imagine how many millions of cardboard boxes this will save.

I am sure today millions of people just open the Amazon box (the brown cardboard one), take out the item in its original box, and then get rid of the shipping box (hopefully everyone recycles, but who knows). So not sure what extra role that extra box plays other than taking more space, wasting more effort, and taking a lot of trees down. Yeah, I know, there is privacy, extra protection, etc. but those are minor, plus that's why I say the website should allow both choices with and w/o extra packaging.

Now finally, some items come in non-cardboard/plastic packaging, which is recyclable only in special facilities, and most of these end up in landfills. Even better reason for providing this option.

posts.stimulus:
Why do people complain that Istio is complicated? It's just a handful of resources, 14 to be precise, including Telemetry, WasmPlugin and the old-school Gateway.

This is to certify that I (still) can type Istio config very fast. In a stressful and less than ideal settings of the exam platform.

On a serious note - I wish I'd done this before working on a large-scale enterprise Istio deployment. I do think Istio's architecture is clean and fairly straightforward. But I've also seen first-hand how wild it gets at scale in real world. What gets lost in the "Istio is too complex" debate is that much of that complexity was already there, the scale and the requirements. Additionally, in many cases Istio was adopted at a time when lot of the functionality which is table stakes today was not available back then.

posts.body_text:
Keith Mattix II if federal judges cannot issue injunctions to prevent federal law from being violated, then what purpose do they serve?

Countless federal judges have issued national injunctions against every administration, including the immediate last one, and up to this point, and the SCOTUS never chose to do anything about it, contest it, or challenge it.

Why pick now, and this issue specifically, to do a 180?

If national injunctions were wrong, they were wrong 10 years ago. 5 years ago. 6 months ago. The SCOTUS has explicitly permitted the practice the entire time. Members of the current majority have upheld the practice in the past.

Should we go back and reverse all the global injunctions federal courts imposed during the Obama era? The Biden era? The previous Trump admin? Even if we should, I think it's pretty clear none of the majority would be interested in doing that.

I don't think it's realistic to pretend that this is a procedural point that's unrelated to the issue that prompted it - if it were, the procedural point would have been resolved decades ago, in one of the hundreds of times it's come up.

posts.body_text:
Benjamin Leggett "If they were wrong, they were wrong 10 years ago. 5 years ago. 6 months ago. The SCOTUS has explicitly permitted the practice the entire time."

Exactly!! - it was always an abuse of judicial power, and it got really pervasive during the Obama administration as they pushed the envelopes of presidential power. Nationwide injunctions are a dangerous form of relief and it turns federal judges into defacto policymakers. Conservatives forum shop so they can get a favorable judge and then get that one judge to change the enforcement of a law nationwide. It needed to stop. 

I'll disagree on the SCOTUS permitting the practice; in the last few terms since Barrett joined the court, there have been several signals in lower-profile cases that nationwide injunctions were on their way out. Do I wish, as a matter of policy, that the Trump administration would be disallowed from enacting their blantantly unconstitutional ban on birthright citizenship? 100%. But the way the founders designed the 3 branches is for the elected officials to face political consequences from the voters. Courts shouldn't get in the way of that. Let people see Trump try to violate the constitution. And then deliver consequences in midterms.

posts.body_text:
Benjamin Leggett I can totally understand why it appears like that. I think from my perspective, the role of SCOTUS is not to handle cases but rather to answer questions. So when a case comes before them (like the migrant rights one you mentioned), they’re not answering “is this good or right or ethical for Trump to do”, they’re answering “is it unconstitutional for the President to do this”

Many legal scholars (not just conservatives, but mostly originalists) are believers in some form of “unitary executive theory”, the idea that all executive power is vested within the President (which is exactly what the constitution says in Article II). So from a first principles perspective, the President has enormous power to change e.g immigration policies as he sees fit. Indeed, the executive branch is the only branch with all of the power vested in a single person.

So in the specific case of these migrant workers, the court is saying “yes Presidents have this power”. I think too often, the American people look to the courts to prevent bad things from happening, but that’s not their function. Their job is to prevent unconstitutional things from happening.

posts.body_text:
Part of the difficulty historically is that pod labels were quickly used as an identity construct in addition to metadata storage; even NetworkPolicy allows using source and destination labels as the basis of user authored policy between services.

If Kubernetes started in 2025, I don’t think we make that mistake. Labels can change during the lifetime of the pod, making them an inappropriate vehicle for identity. Instead, I think something like policy between service accounts would be adopted. 

I very much want to see zero-trust networking become much easier in Kubernetes (that’s why I work on Istio ambient lol), but the APIs of the past constrain us a bit w.r.t solutions. Lior Lieberman and I will be presenting on this topic at maintainer summit during KubeCon EU
posts.stimulus:
If Kubernetes was designed in 2025, would we have zero-trust networking enabled by default for the data plane ?

I posit NOT. In one word - DevEx and in two words - Developer Experience . But there is still hope, keep on reading !

The open-by-default networking model in Kubernetes was an intentional design choice (rather than an afterthought) to encourage adoption and reduce friction:
1⃣ Lift-n-shift workloads (and knowledge) - In a VM world, networking is typically open by default inside a data center
2⃣ Encouraging rapid prototyping - if security policies were too restrictive by default, it would make deploying multiple apps too complex and would kill the adoption
3⃣ Security is not one-size-fits-all - the risk appetite, certificates and secrets compliance and governance varies across different industries and organizations

Docker, Inc won the development war for containers providing a super intuitive and straightforward CLI and ecosystem.
Adding default deny rules and/or identity-based networking to Kubernetes would have been the last nail in its coffin against Docker Swarm or Mesos for winning the production war for container orchestration and deployment.

Remember Pod Security Policies (PSP) and its evolution to Pod Security Standards (PSS)?
I envision something similar for Kubernetes networking as well.

 Here's an alternative design idea (2025 Kubernetes):
Instead of fully open-by-default, Kubernetes could adopt Cloud VM-style networking with different networking profiles, consider this for a baseline:
* Default allow for same-namespace communication (like a VM subnet)
* Default deny for cross-namespace communication (like VPC firewall rules)

And:
* An add-on for mTLS (in the spirit of Gateway API et al.) - via kubectl apply -f

 In 2025, even with all the great OSS/paid solutions out there, zero-trust networking still feels super low-level and we need to know about the existence and high-level mechanics of CNIs / Service Meshes, or policy engines / CEL rules that would generate default deny network policies.

 What do you think? Should Kubernetes introduce built-in security profiles for networking, similar to how Pod Security Standards (PSS) replaced PSP?

#kubernetes #networking #k8s #cni #servicemesh #servicemeshes #kubernetesnetworking #signetwork #cloudnative

posts.body_text:
Keith Mattix II 

The other major overhang here is that 'identity' is just not a standardized thing in networking at all. This issue goes way back before K8S. 

If there was a well supported IETF RFC to ask "who is my peer" the network policy ecosystem would have scaffolded around it and things would be much better. The transports would have adjusted to fulfill its API contract. It's even OK if the answer in highly heterogenous environments was "DONT_KNOW"

 aint it

* Default deny for cross-namespace communication (like VPC firewall rules)

And:
* An add-on for mTLS (in the spirit of Gateway API et al.) - via kubectl apply -f

 In 2025, even with all the great OSS/paid solutions out there, zero-trust networking still feels super low-level and we need to know about the existence and high-level mechanics of CNIs / Service Meshes, or policy engines / CEL rules that would generate default deny network policies.

 What do you think? Should Kubernetes introduce built-in security profiles for networking, similar to how Pod Security Standards (PSS) replaced PSP?

For instance, if we’re building a proof of concept, it might be more important to deliver quickly than to ensure the system can handle millions of users. We can always refine and scale it later if the concept proves successful.

if you own the button in a project and let's say you are still not sure that it may get easily changed during a development process. I’ve been delighting in the grief I’ve received for ending my last post on a cliffhanger. But, for anyone who’s been curious about my career change, I’m happy to announce that I’ve switched to Amazon’s Sustainability Tech organization. 

On the surface, this might not seem like a major change. I’ve already been a TPM at Amazon for more than a decade. 

However, this is the first time in my career I’ve worked on something that directly aligns with my values, as opposed to just being something I appreciate as a customer. Working on Prime Wardrobe’s expansion to third-party sellers was fun because I wear clothes (I’m sure you’re relieved to hear this), and launching that project made it easier for me as a customer to try clothes that I wanted. Working on sustainability hits at a deeper level. 

I’ll be curious to find out how this chapter in my career goes. It’s scarier than I expected working on something I directly care about - I’m not sure I’ll be able to go back. I’m also getting the impression that this is a space where people can burn out quickly. The danger is wanting to change the world NOW and then finding out it’s a longer slog than expected. 

This is another reason I’m grateful for my leave of absence. I’ll never be the world’s most patient person. (This is probably a good trait in a TPM.) However, I’m much better able to deal with short-term frustration for a long-term benefit than I would have been a few months ago. 

Most of all, I’m grateful to be at a company that allows me to keep trying new things sixteen years into my tenure, and work with some hella smart people along the way.

posts.body_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.
posts.stimulus:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

There seems to be a gold rush currently for talent who have experience developing blockchain protocols. 

Keen to understand what these protocols are, and why they are important. I asked ChatGPT to explain what Blockchain Protocols are to me like I was a four-year-old...

"Alright kiddo, imagine you have a big box of Legos. Each Lego block is like a piece of information, such as your favorite toy or a message you want to share. Now, imagine you have a special rulebook for playing with these Legos. This rulebook tells you how to build your Lego tower, who can add new Legos, and how everyone agrees on what the tower looks like.

In the same way, a blockchain protocol is like a super-duper rulebook for a special kind of digital Legos. Instead of Legos, we have blocks of data, like a record of who owns what, or who sent money to whom. The protocol tells everyone how to add new blocks of data, check if they're correct, and agree on what the whole digital tower (or blockchain) looks like.

Protocols make sure everything works smoothly and everyone agrees on what's happening in our digital world." 

If any Blockchain/Protocol Engineers in my network have any thoughts on this, I'd be keen to hear your thoughts!

Nice challenge, I got 13:
```
1. No memset st
2. argv[argc] used wrong, argv[argc-1]
3. not checking the argc > 3 correctly (no default)
4. didn't check stat result
5. buf = (char*)malloc(len) should have len+1 (not counting that the buffer is not zeroed out, since we're gonna read over)
6. printf "Malloc failed!" with len argument not useed by printf
7. not checking fp != NULL after fopen
8. wrong `if (bytes = st.st_size`) instead of `if (bytes != st.st_size)` // fread is guaranteed to read all the bytes if size arg is 1
9. no free(buf)
10. no fclose
11. no break after case 2
12. no zeroing last byte of buf so that printf(%s) works right
13. if file contents contain bytes equal to 0 the print(%s) won't display the whole contents of the file
```

posts.body_text:
That's crazy!
Of course you should move after 2 years.

People who do quit every 2 years get much greater salaries.
"Staying employed at the same company for over two years on average is going to make you earn less over your lifetime by about 50% or more."

They mean over 10 years.
(3%) 1% after inflation raise vs 10-20% raise from moving.

I checked the maths: 
15% raise every 2 years Vs 3% raise every year for 10 years: 
1.15^(5) l / 1.03^(10) = 1.4966

For 30 years: 
1.15^(15) l / 1.03^(30) = 3.35.
So after 30 years, your salary is 235% higher if you change every 2 years!
$100,000 Vs $335,000.

posts.body_text:
People in recent communcations started quoting: "numbers". What are the numbers so often quoted that justify the return to workplace. A junior that doesn't go to the office stagnates in his career if he doesn't make every effort to compensate for that by asking colleagues to work with him. As a senior dev if I spend 3hrs out of my 8hr workday with him I get disqualified for lagging on my own tasks, if my tasks were so easy that I could finish them 3hrs early every day I'd be overqualified.
The problem I'm pointing to is: there needs to be some measure of collaboration, even if vague, and encouragement of that value. A junior seeking collaboration needs to be rewarded as well as his progress in autonomous problem-solving. A senior passing knowledge to others should be rewarded, interest in collaborators' success should be rewarded. Rewards are scarce in the current economic landscape.

posts.body_text: Transparency should be implemented at company level and it should be presented as brackets, not individual salaries. Based on personal merit and impact on company any individual progresses towards the higher part of the bracket until promoted. The first place to start with this transparency should be in job ads though, many times the lack of this transparency encourages personal statistics made by asking friends who don't really do the job you're applying for or searching inaccurate websites causing many people to make bad decisions of either asking too much or too little in interviews. Transparency can also be a motivating factor, to know what one could earn more by doing some specific type of development can make some people have clear career paths early on, provided they have the dedication, ability and if it's also interesting to them.

posts.body_text:
With two consecutive news in the EV and autonomous vehicle industry first
1]BYD a big player from china overtook TESLA for its higher sales of electric vehicles for year 2023.
2] Swaayatt Robots a Indian startup from Bhopal has successfully demonstrated world's first Level 5 autonomous driving capability.
They have automated "Mahindra Bolero" by enabling ADAS technology in it and demonstrated LEVEL 5 ADAS feature of crossing a Toll-Plaza successfully.
You can check the demo here:

So Lets Decode the Levels of ADAS:
What is ADAS?
Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) are technologies designed to enhance vehicle safety by assisting the driver in various tasks. These systems use sensors, cameras, radar, and other technologies to monitor the vehicle's surroundings and provide warnings or take corrective actions when necessary. ADAS can be categorized into different levels based on the level of automation and the extent to which the system can control the vehicle. 

Level 0 - No Automation
At this level, there is no automation, and the driver is fully responsible for controlling the vehicle.
Level 1 - Driver Assistance
Level 1 systems provide a single automated system, such as adaptive cruise control (ACC) or lane-keeping assistance.
The system can either control the vehicle's speed or steering, but not both simultaneously.The driver is required to remain engaged and monitor the vehicle's surroundings.
Level 2 - Partial Automation
Level 2 systems offer a combination of automated features, such as adaptive cruise control and lane-keeping assistance, which can work simultaneously.
The driver is still required to monitor the environment and be ready to take control at any time.
(Tesla's Autopilot is an example of a Level 2 system)
Level 3 - Conditional Automation:
At this level, the vehicle can perform certain driving tasks autonomously under specific conditions.
The driver can disengage from actively controlling the vehicle, but must be ready to take over when the system requests.
(Audi's Traffic Jam Assist is an example of a Level 3 system)
Level 4 - High Automation
Level 4 systems are designed to perform specific driving tasks autonomously without driver intervention in certain scenarios.
The vehicle can operate autonomously within defined conditions, such as highway driving or urban environments.
However, the driver may still need to take control in certain situations.
Level 5 - Full Automation
Level 5 represents full automation, where the vehicle is capable of performing all driving tasks autonomously without any human intervention.
There is no need for a steering wheel or pedals, as the vehicle is fully self-driving.
Level 5 autonomy envisions complete driverless transportation.

Congratulations Swaayatt Robots (स्वायत्त रोबोट्स) for this great milestone.
posts.stimulus:
World's first Level-5 autonomous driving capability demo, where our autonomous vehicle learned to negotiate complex-traffic dynamics in the Toll-Plaza and learned to cross highly unstructured toll-gates. 

This is second only to our Nov-15 2017 and October 22 2023 demos, in terms of Level-5 capabilities, where we enabled #autonomousdriving for negotiating tight-stochastic-dynamic-adversarial environments via multi-RL agents, and bidirectional traffic negotiation on single lane roads, respectively. 

In pursuit of our Level-5 we started asking very tough questions: Can autonomous vehicles learn to cross toll-gates, that may be as unstructured as the ones in India, while also negotiating complex traffic-dynamics in toll-gate areas?

In this demo we showcase for toll-gate crossing and complex traffic negotiation at such intersections, while dealing with the highly stochastic behaviours of large trucks. This is the world's first and the only demo of #autonomousvehicles learning to perform maneuvers of such a complexity.

Our vehicle can be seen entering the toll-gate region through a highway, and then negotiating bidirectional traffic dynamics in an open area with literally no driving rules to adhere to in such regions. Truck drivers often park their trucks randomly at night in Toll-Plaza areas, which can be seen in the video, with numerous trucks parked there. Furthermore, our vehicle had to decide which toll-gate-passage to commit to, while negotiating the criss-cross navigation pattern of the trucks that over-took us.

To understand the level of complexity, a tractor tyre was placed behind a very-large 18-wheeler truck, the detection of which is very tough at night, and our vehicle successfully negotiated it, while a truck was overtaking us from the right.

Finally, our vehicle slowed and paused while approaching a speed-breaker and aligned itself with the toll-gate it committed to on-the-fly, and then crossed the toll-gate while adhering to the driving rule and speed limit at the toll-gate. Furthermore, the gate immediately left to us was barricaded with a broken traffic-police barricade, and our system detected it and adapted accordingly. 

We had developed a very-specific sophisticated motion planning and decision making algorithmic framework to execute this behaviour, and this framework is being further scaled with unsupervised deep learning, and in the month of February we will show an end-to-end negotiation of the day-time traffic.

#reinforcementlearning #deeplearning #machinelearning

posts.stimulus:
Hype Cycle for AI in 2023

Two areas that might be relatively less well-known, but are also quite interesting to check:

◼ Neurosymbolic AI - combines the strengths of symbolic AI and neural networks. Symbolic AI is based on the idea of representing knowledge in a formal way, using symbols and rules based on the use of logic and mathematics.

Neurosymbolic AI uses neural networks to learn the weights of symbolic rules, which allows it to make inferences and draw conclusions in a more precise and accurate way than other approaches, such as machine learning.

Google's LaMDA and DeepMind's AlphaFold are two of the sucessful use cases. LaMDA uses it to understand and generate natural language; AlphaFold uses it to predict the structure of proteins.

◼ First-principles AI / Physics-informed AI - based on the idea that the laws of physics are fundamental and can be used to understand and predict the behavior of the world.

FPAI models are typically built by combining mathematical models of the physical world with ML algorithms. The ML algorithms are used to learn the parameters of the mathematical models, which are then used to make predictions.

Climate modeling, medical diagnosis, energy production, etc, are some potential use cases of FPAI.

Image credit to Gartner

posts.body_text: Ravi Gopinath In case of foreign assets income tax can go back for 16 years also to trigger the provisions of black money act
posts.stimulus:
One of my clients recently received a notice for FY 2018–19 for non-disclosure of Foreign Assets and Income.

What’s alarming?
 Dividend income from a foreign investment was not reported, and the department has invoked the Black Money (Undisclosed Foreign Income and Assets) Act.

Many taxpayers assume that:
Old years are “closed”
Small foreign dividends don’t matter
AIS is not showing the same
That assumption is costly.

Foreign assets, even with minor income, must be disclosed every year once acquired. Non-disclosure can trigger severe penalties, irrespective of the amount involved.

 Key takeaway:
 If you have ever held foreign shares, ESOPs, RSUs, ETFs, bank accounts, or earned foreign dividends — review your past filings now, not after a notice arrives.
Compliance delayed is often compliance denied.

Notice under Section 10(1) of the Black Money (Undisclosed Foreign Income and Assets) and Imposition of Tax Act, 2015

Stay safe. Stay disclosed.

posts.body_text: Is fatty level disease reversible? What can be done to reverse it? Dr. Manan Vora
posts.stimulus:
84% of IT employees in India have a damaged liver — and most don’t even know it.

A new study from the University of Hyderabad confirms Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Fatty Liver Disease (MAFLD) is now an epidemic in India’s IT sector:

- 84% of IT employees are already affected.
- 71% are clinically obese.
- 34% have metabolic syndrome (diabetes, high BP, abnormal cholesterol)

This is a lifestyle disease, and you should be concerned if you follow these habits:

 Sitting 8+ hours daily

 Sleeping <6 hours everyday

 Consuming 2 sugary drinks/week (+45% risk)

 Increasing belly fat - Waist over 90 cm (men) or 80 cm (women)

Fatty liver progresses silently until it's too late, potentially leading to:

- Liver inflammation → Chronic damage
- Fibrosis → Scarring of liver tissue
- Cirrhosis → Permanent damage requiring a liver transplant
- Liver cancer → The final, deadly stage

As a doctor, I wish more people realise this — Your deadlines, meetings, and late-night grind WILL NOT matter if your health fails.

So fix it before it’s too late:

▶︎ 1. Get a liver check-up (ultrasound & LFT detect early damage)

▶︎ 2. Avoid sugar & refined carbs when you can (Moderation is key)

▶︎ 3. Move every hour (even 5-minute breaks help)

▶︎ 4. Prioritise protein & fibre (helps burn excess liver fat)

▶︎ 5. Fix your sleep (sleep deprivation damages liver more than alcohol)

Most people only realise the value of health when they lose it. But don't wait until your body forces you to stop.

If you found this helpful, please repost this. Someone in your network needs to read this today.

#healthandwellness #workplacehealth #fitness

posts.stimulus:
For the love of everything that is holy in the world, please PLEASE take time to personalize your GPT model so it can sound like you, and my feed can look like actual people wrote it— and make me want to read your content!

It’s not you, it’s your GPT!

To make content yours, avoid/delete/rewrite (if at all possible):

• Contrast framing (the classic “not X, but Y” such as “This isn’t a feature, it’s a movement”
• Purpose compression (short sentences that reduce complexity into intent, such as “The signal is clear”)
• Minimalism that feels engineered (Statements such as “designed to listen”, or “trust, operationalized”)

Above all, if you were never a writer before and are now posting best-selling, philosophical content that is not aligned with your brand, please stop!

I really do want to engage with your content. As long as it sounds like it’s YOURS!

Brand it! Please brand it. It takes one minute!

Miri

#AIWorldProblems #Stop

posts.stimulus:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #AILeadership #GrannyCoreIsPowerCore #BayAreaTech #SisterhoodAndStrategy

Yesterday marked the final day of my Explore (SWE + TPM) Internship at Xbox! 

This summer was an incredible journey of learning and growth. I joined the Experiences and Platforms for Developers: Creator Onboarding, Resources, and Support (XPD-CORS) team, contributing to a greenfield project aimed at transforming how Xbox supports creators in play testing their games.

As a TPM, I led partner studio discussions, authored a feature spec, and collaborated across time zones with a designer to produce Figma designs.

As a SWE, I designed, built, and deployed the backend service, API, and data layer for an end-to-end proof of concept, successfully demoing it to XPD multiple times.

Other highlights included placing 3rd in the Intern Celebration Hackathon, earning 2nd in Halo Reach: Firefight during the Xbox Intern Gaming Tournament, winning a tour of Mojang Studios!

I also played a ton of Sand Volleyball and started reading One Piece

I’m deeply grateful to those who made this internship unforgettable:
 My pod, the ThunderDome — Sunday Ochigbo and Raul Ballines (FIFA Fridays forever, trust).
 My manager Suvir Arora and (formerly) Alex Blount 
 My mentors Brian Trevethan and Greg Gonyea

All the challenges, guidance, encouragement, and camaraderie made my first internship a truly unforgettable one.

posts.body_text:
The GitHub Copilot SDK has jump-started the imagination and creativity of many, including Ve Sharma, Software Solution Engineer at Microsoft (). Loving all the use cases emerging from the technical preview. 

Here’s a link to the full post about Building Context-Aware CI with GitHub Copilot SDK and Microsoft WorklQ

#github #githubactions #platformengineering #agenticsdlc #aidev #githubcopilot
posts.stimulus:
I Built Context-Aware CI with GitHub Copilot SDK and Microsoft WorkIQ - it's the ability to catch code issues that violate what was said in a meeting, email, etc.

We've all seen it: 2-hour planning meeting, team agrees on PostgreSQL, then someone opens a PR adding MongoDB two weeks later. 40 hours of rework.

The root cause? 

Context fragmentation. Decisions live in Teams transcripts, emails, and "someone's head"—but CI only knows code.

What Microsoft + GitHub makes possible:
An integrated, end-to-end business layer for development context. WorkIQ surfaces your M365 data (meetings, emails, Teams, documents). The GitHub Copilot SDK provides the reasoning & agentic engine. 

Together, they close the loop between organizational decisions and code automatically.

I built a proof-of-concept that changes this:

GitHub Copilot SDK + Microsoft WorkIQ = CI pipeline that validates code PRs against your team's actual decisions.

 Developer wants to merge code via PR
 CI extracts context, queries relevant meeting transcripts
 Copilot SDK analyzes diff against decisions
 Three-state outcome: PASS / WARN / FAIL
 PR comment outline the violations to specific meetings as an example.

The result? 

Rework incidents drop. 

Reviews focus on logic, not policy. Institutional knowledge becomes enforceable not just documented.

The future of agentic devOps & CI isn't just testing code — it's validating intent.

 And it's only possible when your business context and the dev toolchain are truly integrated.

My full technical writeup with implementation details is in the thread below

posts.stimulus:
WE’RE IN FORBES 

Mum, I think we made it.

Waking up last week and seeing Juno featured in Forbes felt surreal.

Just 6 months ago, Juno didn’t exist. It was just Isaac Tolley and me working on a research project at Oxford and UCL, trying to improve life for people living with chronic illness.

We knew the healthcare system was broken. 1B people live with chronic disease.

Most spend years waiting for a diagnosis and without any form of support.

At Juno, we’re changing that. How?

→ Supporting patients 24/7, not just during a 10-minute appointment
→ Analysing millions of parameters from each individual’s health history, every single day
→ Cutting diagnosis time from years to months

We’ve already helped support over 30,000 people - and we’re just getting started…

posts.body_text: Daniel Semedo, desejo muito sucesso em sua nova jornada. Foi um prazer ter trabalhado com você, sempre solícito, alegre e com grande profissionalismo.
posts.stimulus:
Encerro um ciclo de 13 anos na Microsoft, uma jornada que me trouxe aprendizados imensos, grandes realizações e a oportunidade de viver o sonho de trabalhar em uma das maiores empresas de tecnologia do mundo. Tive experiências que ultrapassaram fronteiras, colaborando com clientes e colegas em projetos no Brasil e no exterior. Foi um privilégio participar na linha de frente de momentos marcantes e escrever uma parte da história da Microsoft, como a liderança em Inteligência Artificial, o fortalecimento e expansão do Azure e a evolução da engenharia de software através do Visual Studio, Azure DevOps e posteriormente com GitHub, impactando centenas de milhares de desenvolvedores ao longo desses anos nos quais tive oportunidade de visitar, conhecer e conversar.

Nada disso teria sido possível sem as pessoas incríveis que estiveram ao meu lado nessa trajetória. Sou profundamente grato a todos que me apoiaram, inspiraram e me ensinaram em cada projeto, conversa e desafio. Além de colegas, fiz amigos para a vida toda e sou grato por cada história vivida juntos.

Chegou o momento de iniciar uma nova jornada, ela será interessante e diferente do que já trabalhei, uma boa mudança que trará experiência complementar de tudo que vivi nesses 13 anos de Microsoft, mas essas novidades deixarei para uma próxima história (post)!

Yesterday, 

One 23 yr old sacrificed his precious life for our motherland. Our young Army officer Lt #RishiKumar & Sepoy #ManjitSingh lost their precious lives in an Improvised Explosive Devise (IED) blast near a forward post along the Line of Control (LoC) in Nowshera sector of Jammu and Kashmir’s Rajouri district on Saturday.

जब हम बैठे थे घरों में, वोह झेल रहे थे गोली l

One 23 yr old super rich guy (from a privileged family, courtesy Indians who support mediocre quality escapist-puppy love popcorn films) came back home on bail, with full-fanfare, after 20+ days long existence in a jail, in a drug case. The welcome given to this guy was loud and celebratory as if he had won the Kargil war. Most of us were appalled to see the crowds gathered here.

Guess, who did the Indian media highlight as the headliner? The latter. Who did Indians like us thought of and extended our prayers to? The former. While the media in India busy eulogizing the 23 yr drug accused son of a Bollywood star, most of us were sad for our martyrs, the 23 yr old Lieutenant and the sepoy; we have long been feeling quite uncomfortable with the loss of young patriotic Indians at the borders, on a regular basis. 

Besides this, I am sure, most of us are bothered by something : the Indian media (which still has the money bags and reach to influence young and old minds) and their blatant preference for rich and famous, divisive and wokes, and their incapacity to regard our soldiers, to raise debates/discussions on every crucial issue - from the still under-developed primary education facilities in the country to child-trafficking to lack of proper roads in the remotest parts of India where teachers cover miles & cross rivers carrying mid-day meals ration for their school children to everything that needs a rapid attention.

Really? Is our India-her culture that small to be equated with a star?

Swati Jhawar I thoroughly enjoy reading your short, yet insightful content. Love how you talked about no explanation and no guilt - hyper focus. This is one of the hardest things to do as sacrifice and "selflessness" was glorified, growing up. 
Would love to hear more on this and how you do it !!
posts.stimulus:
I’m building big things while trying not to miss small yet precious moments.
I live by one principle: protect the day in front of me, win it, then repeat.

Right now, my life runs on parallel tracks.
- A full-time job where I put my best foot forward, staying hands-on with AI evolution.
- A real estate business scaling towards a dream I’ve carried growing up: Belle Vie Estate riverfront estate designed for private multi-day celebrations.
- Two young kids (6 and 3) who need presence and quality time, not leftovers.
- Showing up for parents, siblings, and family.
- Planning financially for a secure future.
- And in between all those: bedtime stories, school projects, after school clubs, healthy meals, hikes, workouts, summer planning, list could go on and on.

Twenty-four hours is painfully short at this stage.
But I don’t overthink “balance.” I run one day at a time.

My rule is simple: start the day with 3 outcomes to achieve and cut everything that doesn’t serve them.
I say No to everyone and everything that doesn’t fit. No explanations. No guilt spirals. Just a clear tradeoff.
This rule doesn’t shrink my dreams. It protects them. It helps me focus and keeps my head clear.

Each morning, I ask: Why this? Why now?
If it doesn’t serve today’s 3 outcomes, it’s out.
That’s how I stay present with my kids (non-negotiable) and keep momentum on the all the other tracks, even when the “how” isn’t fully clear yet.

Prioritize the present. Move the intent. Keep going.
Clarity has a way of catching up to momentum.
I remind myself; success isn’t just the finish. It’s the distance you keep traveling, especially on hard days.

Today’s outcomes included Super Bowl watch party readiness. GO HAWKS!

#LifeLessons #WorkDreamLifeIntegration #Enterpreneurship #Parenting #RuthlessPrioritization #MentalHealth #WinADay #StackWins

posts.stimulus:
“How do some people do so much in one life?"

My six-year-old asked me this during a 30-minute Waymo ride. Instead of talking about productivity, time management, and other loaded concepts, I pulled out my favorite Hindi poem “Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti.” Those who try never really lose.

It starts with the tiny ant, dragging a grain up a wall. It keeps slipping and falling, but its inner belief fills it with courage, so it returns to the same wall again and again. The effort looks small, but the lesson is huge: if you keep moving, the wall eventually runs out of height. Then a diver, taking repeated dips into the ocean and coming back with empty hands, because pearls do not sit in shallow water. They keep going deeper, knowing that some rewards only appear for people who can handle many empty returns. 

The poem emphasizes perseverance and continuous effort. Failure is a challenge; accept it. See what was lacking and improve it. Give up some comfort, but do not run away from the battlefield of struggle. 

As we reached our destination, I pointed to the Waymo itself. If humanity had not embraced this mindset, we might have stopped at walking. No carts, no cars, no planes, no electric vehicles, and certainly no driverless ride where we can spend 30 minutes on poetry instead of traffic.

To bring it home for him, I reminded him of our hospitality journey. Despite working full time, his parents (Rahil & I) strive to grow our hospitality business Kēsar Vineyards and Events to new heights, not because it is easy but without effort, we will not discover what is possible for our family or the guests we want to serve.

I left him with a question: what is one area you have thought “not for me” that deserves one more honest try this month? Treat every stumble as the ant’s slip or the diver’s empty hand, not as the final verdict.
#growthmindset #grit #parenting #entrepreneurship #hospitality #learning #resilience

posts.stimulus:
How I built a habit that prevents quitting? 

I developed this as a child. Protect yesterday’s win, then raise the ceiling.

It began in school. Two months without an absence became a quarter, then a year. From sixth through tenth grade I did not miss a day. The streak made the choice each morning. Protect the win. Add one.

That rule shaped bigger moves. I chose engineering and left home after tenth grade to build my own path. Received first US patent, then added more. Same pattern reflected in other aspects. Thirteen years of clean driving record. Sixteen years at Microsoft with strong peer relationships and consistent recognition. The goal was not tenure. The goal was to continuously raise yesterday’s baseline. If 30 pushups is the edge, I do 31. One extra rep turns a ceiling into a floor.

Outside of work, Hospitality Entrepreneurship tested the pattern. Seven years ago we (Rahil and I) converted our garage into a studio and hosted our first guests. Then more condos. Then a vineyard stay. Now we are building Kēsar Vineyards and Events a specialized multi-day wedding venue. Same rule. Keep the last win alive. Stretch it slightly. Then add one to create new personal baseline. This weekend we drove 5hrs to shadow a renowned event and wedding planner during a live event and study the craft up close. New input. New higher standard.

I’m sharing because many of us want a habit that prevents quitting. This is mine. I treat yesterday’s small win as the floor. I refuse to break the floor. Then I add one. I rely on my system: small streaks that become identity, and identity that makes the next action non-negotiable. Tiny on any given day. Transformative across a season.

If you want to try it for 30 days: pick one behavior that matters, set the smallest version you can keep on your worst day, track one visible receipt, protect the floor, then add one. #CompoundingEffort #TheStreakIsTheStrategy #IntrinsicMotivation

R.I.P. The "Easy Apply" button. 

Claude 4.5 Opus just made it obsolete.

I’ve stress-tested pretty much every AI model for writing cold outreach: GPT-4, Grok, Llama, the whole lineup.

They all fail the same way.

You can smell the AI from the first line.

“Hope you’re doing well…” 

Then I tried Claude 4.5 and it was the first time an LLM didn’t sound like it was pretending to be human.

It actually understands professional dynamics.

Not just what to say, but why you’d say it.

I work closely with hiring teams, so I know exactly which messages get ignored and which ones spark real conversations. I spent ~5 hours dialing in a prompt that makes Claude 4.5 write networking DMs that consistently get responses.

What changed?
 Messages that read like they came from a peer
 Hooks grounded in real company context (no hallucinations)
 Questions recruiters want to answer
 Zero “please hire me” energy

The system does three things extremely well:
 → Breaks down the JD + your actual experience
 → Maps your skills to the team’s unspoken pain points
 → Crafts a “warm intro” that feels unmistakably human

I’m sharing the exact Claude 4.5 networking prompt plus a short guide on how to use it.

If you’re still relying on generic cover letters or hoping Easy Apply works someday… this is a much faster path to the top of the pile.

Comment “OPUS” below (and make sure we’re connected so I can DM you) and I’ll send it over for free.

posts.stimulus:
I'm a little late to the party, but I finally put AI to the test on a real coding project.

The project is a support tool for our development team, so low risk if something went wrong. I asked Claude to improve some WPF canvas code by adding an animated antenna to represent signals from an offshore vessel, along with click events.

It took several iterations. Sometimes the code didn’t compile, sometimes I fixed the bugs myself, and sometimes I just made tweaks by hand (which was faster than re-prompting AI). In the end, the antenna looked good. The code was a bit messy, but then again, the original codebase wasn't exactly pretty either, so maybe the AI was just following our conventions. 

My takeaways from this first round of AI-assisted coding:

- Great for exploration. It surfaced APIs and language features I hadn’t seen before.

- Not great for polish. Expect to refactor if you care about clean code.

- Less useful when you already know the solution. Typing has never been the bottleneck.

The biggest time saver was discovering parts of the WPF Canvas API I didn’t know. Normally, I’d have learned this more slowly through searches and documentation. Still, I believe it's important to take time to study those new concepts that AI introduces us to. Otherwise we aren't really qualified to evaluate the quality of what AI produces.

For me, AI fit neatly into the early phase of problem-solving: piecing together a solution while expanding my knowledge. Once I understood the problem and knew what "good" looked like, it was easier to take over, clean things up, and bring the code up to company standards. Once the solution became clear, prompting AI further felt less like engineering and more like pulling a slot machine lever.

I know I still have a lot to learn in this new programming paradigm. How does this line up with your experience?

posts.stimulus:
Top 10 Database Scaling Techniques You Should Know:

1. Indexing: Create indexes on frequently queried columns to speed up data retrieval.

2. Vertical Scaling: Upgrade your database server by adding more CPU, RAM, or storage to handle increased load.

3. Caching: Store frequently accessed data in-memory (e.g., Redis) to reduce database load and improve response time.

4. Sharding: Distribute data across multiple servers by splitting the database into smaller, independent shards, allowing for horizontal scaling and improved performance.

5. Replication: Create multiple copies (replicas) of the database across different servers, enabling read queries to be distributed across replicas and improving availability.

6. Query Optimization: Fine-tune SQL queries, eliminate expensive operations, and leverage indexes effectively to improve execution speed and reduce database load.

7. Connection Pooling: Reduce the overhead of opening/closing database connections by reusing existing ones, improving performance under heavy traffic.

8. Vertical Partitioning: Split large tables into smaller, more manageable parts (partitions), each containing a subset of the columns from the original table.

9. Denormalization: Store data in a redundant but structured format to minimize complex joins and speed up read-heavy workloads.

10. Materialized Views: Pre-compute and store results of complex queries as separate tables to avoid expensive recalculation, reducing database load and improving response times.

The way I did is, go through the project and understand the current coding style and implement a change to match the current coding standards.
posts.stimulus:
Recently, I thought of using AI to help with some production work. The task was fairly simple — I needed to update an existing CDK package to add a new S3 stack.
I didn’t spend much time diving into the codebase; instead, I asked the AI to analyze the repo and implement the required changes. Initially, it did a great job — it generated code that looked correct at first glance. However, when I tried to build it, I faced some challenges. With a few prompt iterations and by passing the error logs, the AI was eventually able to fix the bugs, and the build succeeded.

Two days later, I realized that the new S3 stack was missing permissions, so I had to add them manually. While reviewing the existing code, I discovered something surprising: the entire CDK codebase was structured using the Factory Pattern. Ideally, the correct way to add the new stack was to create a new factory. Instead, the AI had created the stack differently, ignoring the established pattern.
Now, I have to raise a new change request — removing the AI-generated code and rewriting it properly using the factory approach. 

My two cents:
Using AI to generate code can be powerful and time-saving, but if you don’t understand the underlying code architecture, the solution may not align with your team’s standards or be scalable. Also, when things break, debugging AI-generated code can take longer than fixing bugs in code you wrote yourself.
