posts.body_text: Bill Karwin I thought you, "... preferred not to practice the same bad behavior that they did."?
posts.stimulus:
The worst interviewer I ever had:

He swaggered into the room and sat down heavily in his chair. “I just have one question for you,” were his first words. “What’s the difference between Java and C++?”

I described some differences, like garbage collection and generics, and the fact that Java is a proprietary language whereas C++ is an ANSI standard, Java compilers generate bytecode, whereas C++ compilers usually generate native machine code, and so on.

“WRONG!” he roared. “There’s no difference! They’re exactly the same language!” Then he sat back, obviously satisfied at having demonstrated his superiority.

I wanted to ask him, “What did you think you would learn about me as a candidate by asking that question?”

Or, “If I accept a job at this company, would I ever have to talk to you again?”

But I just nodded pleasantly and waited for him to pass me to the next interviewer. I did not complain to the company about him. What would be the point? Did I hope to persuade the company to fire him somehow? If I still wanted the job (which is doubtful), would complaining about an interviewer be the best way to make that happen?

If you have a bad feeling about an interviewer, assume that working for that person will be terrible. Politely finish the interview and never look back.

Complaining about an arrogant interviewer has virtually no upside for you, and is much more likely to demonstrate that you’re just as big an asshole as the interviewer was.

Over the last few years working as a software engineer, I became embarrassed mentioning these few things:

1. It gets lonely - Working as an adult taking care of home chores and other responsibilities doesn't give a lot of room to make friends. It hasn't been easy moving to another state for the sake of work.

2. I'm insecure about my level and compensation - Even working at Google doesn't make the feeling stop. I started comparing myself with new grad engineers with similar compensation and senior engineers who reached their level with less work experience.

3. Having a full-time job doesn't feel like enough - At 26, it feels like I should be doing more instead of spending my days as a software engineer at Google. There's others in YCombinator, making YT Videos, and starting non-profits.

4. I keep asking myself if I'm happy - I don't know if I want to climb the corporate ladder, spend more time with family, or invest in hobbies. There's pro's and con's to all of them and it's never been an easy decision to make.

These are some of the things I started thinking about more in my late 20's.

posts.body_text:
Stefan de Kooter - Great Question!

For direct VNet-to-VNet connectivity, we recommend Vnet Peering or Azure Virtual Network Manager Meshes.
 
In this case, Routing Appliance is primarily intended for customers who already rely on NVAs due to their existing network topologies. These customers often find themselves limited by throughput bottlenecks and are looking for a way to scale up to multiple tbps bandwidth. The current implementation supports routing (via UDRs), access control (via NSGs) and Private Links.
 
As we move towards general availability (GA), we will be adding additional features to support broader networking use cases. Also remember this is still a public preview and we are looking to get feedback from everyone on the product.
posts.stimulus:
Virtual Network Routing Appliance ("Routing Appliance") Public Preview!

During Ignite, I posted about exciting developments in building a high-performance, native appliance in Azure that will help customers achieve low-latency, high-throughput routing without the need of a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA). Today I'm happy to announce that we have released a public preview of this feature. I hope you get a chance to try it out and we are looking to build many more exciting capabilities on top of this new technology.

NVAs have been the backbone of complex Azure networking topologies where customers needed to route traffic through a central point. While there are still many use cases for NVAs, such as advanced firewalls, for many scenarios NVAs just added complexity but were required to provide a route point. NVAs require the deployment of a virtual machine, route traffic through the guest OS, require the additional overhead of OS management and can create a bottleneck in terms of performance.

Today, with the announcement of the Routing Appliance you get configurable "Azure native" route point with networking speeds up to 200Gbps. You get the management ease of any Azure resource. No need to deploy or configure an operating system. With just a few clicks and an Azure resource deployment, you have a high scale route enabled appliance with native support for Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs) giving you easy traffic filtering and routing.

As I said in a previous post, today is exciting to have 2 great announcements. This is the second one, where I am so excited to see my sibling team release Network Security Perimeter (NSP) into General Availability! I have had the great privilege to work with this amazing team as they have built a complimentary feature to Azure Virtual Network Manager in the PaaS space. While not my direct team or product, NSP team is my closest peer and I've had the great opportunity to work with them on their design, share code, and build great ideas together. It's a great day to celebrate their achievement and bringing an amazing product to the Azure Portfolio.

Network security perimeter allows organizations to define a logical network isolation boundary for PaaS resources (for example, Azure Storage account and SQL Database server) that are deployed outside your organization’s virtual networks. It restricts public network access to PaaS resources within the perimeter; access can be exempted by using explicit access rules for public inbound and outbound. 

Features of a network security perimeter include: 
* Resource to resource access communication within perimeter members, preventing data exfiltration to non-authorized destinations. 
* External public access management with explicit rules for PaaS resources associated with the perimeter. 
* Access logs for audit and compliance. 
* Unified experience across PaaS resources. 

Learn more about NSP: 

One great example of using NSP is with Azure Monitor:

posts.stimulus:
One of the most asked for features by customers for Azure Virtual Network Manager (AvNM) isn't a feature at all! It was to change our billing model. 

Previously, AvNM billed based on subscriptions with managed virtual networks. However, this model didn't align with Azure best practices that recommend having a larger number of subscriptions. For many customers, this blocked adoption.

Now, we bill directly based on the virtual networks managed. This model of billing aligns with the actual resources under management and gives a more accurate bill for our customers. 

I'm pleased to announce that all Azure public regions now have this new billing model and all future network managers will be billed based on virtual networks.

Aidan Finn, I know you have been a big champion of this and special thanks for your ongoing support and feedback!

posts.stimulus:
IP Address Management (IPAM)! 

It's something that I remember doing 20 years ago in an Excel spreadsheet. And many companies today still fall back to this tried-and-true method for managing their IP address allocations. 

However, this week I'm really excited to announce that Azure Virtual Network Manager IPAM is in public preview.

Now customers can create IP address pools, child pools to help organize, and then allocate Azure Virtual Networks directly from these address pools. You also can keep track of all your on-premises allocations! No more accidental overlapping of IP address spaces between virtual networks or with on-prem systems. 

Create a Network Manager ⇨ Create your Pool ⇨ Ready to Go
Create a Virtual Network ⇨ Now pick an allocation pool for your IP space

Azure Virtual Network Manager (AVNM) Fridays: Connectivity 
I’m excited to dive into a series on a fantastic product I’ve been working on – Azure Virtual Network Manager. Every week, I’ll share a piece of AVNM’s magic – after a brief hiatus, we’re now diving into connectivity configurations!
Check out my previous AVNM Fridays posts covering the basics and security admin rules:
 
 

 What is an AVNM connectivity configuration?
In AVNM, connectivity configurations are how you build and manage your network topologies at scale. In a connectivity config, you specify your desired network topology, which network groups should be a part of that topology, and some additional options (like establishing direct connectivity among the members of a network group). Then you can deploy your connectivity configs to whichever regions you want! AVNM can even clean up your old existing peerings after config deployment.
For your config’s topology, you can choose between mesh, hub and spoke, and hub and spoke with direct connectivity (think mini-mesh among each network groups’ respective members). AVNM also provides a visualization tool from Azure Resource Topology so you can see the connectivity your config is creating among network groups and their member VNets!

 Use cases
So, what can I do with AVNM’s connectivity configurations?
o Connect VNets across subscriptions and regions in just a few clicks.
o Leverage central infrastructure services in a hub VNet with other VNets.
--> Establish direct connectivity among spoke VNets to reduce latency.
--> For example, you can create a hub and spoke topology with all of your production and test VNets connected to the same hub VNet, and enable direct connectivity among only your production VNets – all in the same config. 
o Automatically maintain connectivity at scale, even with the addition of new network resources.
--> Defining your network groups’ membership with Azure Policy removes the manual overhead of ensuring your new and existing VNets are connected as intended.

Where can I learn more?

Azure blog on AVNM use cases:

Azure Virtual Network Manager (AVNM) Fridays: The Basics 
I’m excited to dive into a series on a fantastic product I’ve been working on – Azure Virtual Network Manager. Every week, I’ll share a piece of AVNM’s magic – and today, we’ll start with the basics!

Whether you're a seasoned cloud pro or just starting your cloud journey, AVNM is a game-changer in managing your Azure network resources globally and at scale. Today, the AVNM service is generally available, along with its hub and spoke connectivity configuration and security admin configuration (in 30 regions and counting).

 What is AVNM and how does it work?
Azure Virtual Network Manager is a centralized network management solution that helps you manage the connectivity and security of your network resources. Once you create your network manager resource, putting this into action only takes 3 main steps:
 1⃣ Group your network resources however you see fit. You can even automatically group your resources by defining conditions.
 2⃣ Create your connectivity and security admin configurations to achieve your desired topology and enforce organization-level policies.
 3⃣ Deploy your configurations onto your network groups in any regions you want.

 Key benefits
So, what makes AVNM stand out? 
 · Centralized controls --> simplified global management across regions and subscriptions.
 · Automatic configuration application on your network resources --> connectivity maintenance and day-zero protection, even for new resources.
 · Enforced security rules across your organization’s new and existing network resources --> mitigate risk of changes or security holes.
 · Regional deployment of configuration changes --> safely test in regions, fix, & roll forward.

Where can I learn more?

posts.body_text:
Ah yes - this is quite a common limiting belief - that the leader is there to dispense wisdom and to guide folks through their career. 

It assumes a mentor role for the leader that doesn’t need to exist, and very few software leaders can actually fulfill, anyway.

The leader is there to lead the development of software - this means setting up systems, ensuring the people are of the right caliber, and ensuring that the teams that the people form into have the skills to be and actually are autonomous. This is a full-time job, and one that only the leader can do.

Having a bunch of regular one-on-ones and pretending to be a mentor that you likely aren’t unless your team is all super junior, while it may be gratifying, isn’t leading.

Some strategic mentoring and individual value-add is important, don’t get me wrong - when it’s actually possible. But scheduling it super-regularly is not only self-deception, it’s a massive waste of time and a dereliction of duty.

You talk about hiring too many junior developers but there is a very small pool of talented seniors out there. Secondly juniors don't become seniors without mentorship and guidance. My job is to ensure my team grows and my juniors become competent self-managing engineers. I purposely give tasks that I could do in 2 days to a dev that will take weeks to complete so they can grow.

Secondly Devops builds better code in my opinion. After waking up at 3am a few times due to a live site issue most devs quickly learn the importance of releasing well tested and quality code.
posts.stimulus:
Forcing a software team to reach outside itself for any technical concern will limit the value the team is creating, and it will limit the organization’s ability to scale. 

Architecture Teams, “DevOps” Teams, Frontend/Backend Teams, DBA Teams - these are all ill-advised waste. True concern about organizational value creation will put eliminating these albatross at the top of the to-do list.

A common root problem that can lead to their formation is simply hiring too junior software developers. Another is mistakenly transferring the idea of economy of scale from an Industrial Age setting to an Information Age one. You may need to remedy these dysfunctions before streamlining your team.

#softwaredevelopment #leadershipcraftsmanship #informationage
topics_ordered: recognition of contributions in team settings

posts.body_text:
I don't disagree with your first two points; the pool of qualified software developers is small, and junior developers need mentoring. Both very true. 

Your third point regarding "devops" is a function of the first two - which is what I was trying to get across in the post - since folks are hiring teams that are too junior, all of their code is poorer than it could/should be, devops code included.

There's nothing inherent to an external team that leads to better code. It's the fact that the engineers are more versed in what they are doing.

5 dokaza da je Rajder iz Patrolnih šapa toksični mikromenadžer:

1. Ne vrednuje tuđe vreme. Na sastanke saziva sve članove tima, umesto samo one koji će učestvovati u akciji.

2. Govori drugima šta da rade, umesto da njima to prepusti. Kao da Rabl ne zna šta treba da radi kašikom bagera ili Skaj kukom helikoptera.

3. Favorizuje određene članove tima. U ogromnom broju akcija učestvuju Čejs i Skaj, dok Maršal dođe na red u svakoj 10. epizodi (čekali smo pet dana da čujemo koja je Maršalova rečenica kad ga Rajder prozove)

4. Ne poštuje slobodno vreme članova svog tima. Često ih zove čak i po mraku, očekujući da se oni momentalno odazovu. To stvara ogroman stres kod Šapa.

5. Daje klijentima obećanja bez prethodne saglasnosti članova svog tima. Svakom ko ga nazove kaže da odmah dolaze i da će biti rešeno, čime Šapama ne ostavlja nimalo prostora za pregovore i stalno ih baca u vatru.

posts.stimulus:
I would have never thought that creating a simple text prediction software is that easy. After watching one of the Veritasium videos on Markov chains I got inspired by the story how Markov himself created manual and tedios "network" based on the text of Eugene Onegin by Pushkin. I was suspicious that such a simple model with just checking last few charachers can produce text that, of course being non-sensical, seems very legit on the first glance.

So, I've spent an hour of so implementing this in C++, fed it with some English texts and I've got some really decent results, for example, looking like this:

"That wild yell seemed to come a violent puff of wind, and the door to the winding stair blew to with a shock that set the dust from the lintels flying. When I ran to push it open, I found that it was hopelessly fast. I was again a prisoner, and that if I wished it I could have no choice. The Count saw his victory in my bow, and his mastery in the trouble of my face, for he said almost joyously:, you don't count now; the Master is at hand. The attendant thinks it is some sudden form of religious mania at once might be angerous. I got a ladder myself, and crossing the wall, dropped down on the other side of a true love episode of which I know one side already...."

The C++ code is as simple as this 

Anyways, real LLMs are way more complex and they base their predicitons on words, or more preciely tokens and have multiple layers of decision makings. However, a simple code like this can still produce interesting results.

posts.body_text: Apsolutno se slažem. Meni je isto ovo zapadalo za oči dok sam ih svojevremeno gledao i uvek mi je to smetalo. Nadam se da će ova poruka stići do Rajdera i da će biti bolji prema kucama.
posts.stimulus:
5 dokaza da je Rajder iz Patrolnih šapa toksični mikromenadžer:

1. Ne vrednuje tuđe vreme. Na sastanke saziva sve članove tima, umesto samo one koji će učestvovati u akciji.

2. Govori drugima šta da rade, umesto da njima to prepusti. Kao da Rabl ne zna šta treba da radi kašikom bagera ili Skaj kukom helikoptera.

3. Favorizuje određene članove tima. U ogromnom broju akcija učestvuju Čejs i Skaj, dok Maršal dođe na red u svakoj 10. epizodi (čekali smo pet dana da čujemo koja je Maršalova rečenica kad ga Rajder prozove)

4. Ne poštuje slobodno vreme članova svog tima. Često ih zove čak i po mraku, očekujući da se oni momentalno odazovu. To stvara ogroman stres kod Šapa.

5. Daje klijentima obećanja bez prethodne saglasnosti članova svog tima. Svakom ko ga nazove kaže da odmah dolaze i da će biti rešeno, čime Šapama ne ostavlja nimalo prostora za pregovore i stalno ih baca u vatru.

posts.body_text: U principu tačno. Teško je ukomponovati da ljudi koji rade zajedno baš sede jedni pored drugih, možda i nemoguče. Danas radim sa jednim kolegom i saradjujem dosta, a za dve nedelje će mi glavni saradnik biti neko potuno drugi.
posts.stimulus:
Zašto je open space sr…nje? Ili u prevodu, kada mogu da dobijem bolje slušalice?

Petnaest godina sam vodio IT kompanije.
Deset godina pre toga radio sam u njima, kao inženjer.
Za sve to vreme nagledao sam se različitih kancelarijskih formata, ali jedna tema se nike menjala, open space. Uvek je imao istu priču: povećaće kolaboraciju, ljudi će više razgovarati, biće otvoreniji jedni prema drugima i efikasnost rada na projektima će porasti.

Ali stvarnost je potpuno drugačija. Da li mogu da dobijem bolje slušalice i mikrofon koji izoluje buku, da li mogu da se premestim u drugu kancelariju, da li mogu intervju sa klijentom da odradim od kuće, potreban mi je fokus. 

U tim prostorima gde bi trebalo da se „svi čuju“, ljudi se sve manje slušaju.
Sede jedni pored drugih, a ne kažu ni „dobro jutro“. Kada sam prvi put to video davne 2008.godine, nisam mogao da verujem. Sedim u open space kancelariji za pedesetak ljudi, u drugom gradu, i gledam, ulaze jedan po jedan, i samo re retki javljaju jedni drugima. Oni koji sede jedni do drugih, naravno, najšešće.
Neki ne znaju imena svojih kolega, niti imaju predstavu ko uopšte radi sa kojim tehnologijama i na kojim projektima.
Zamišljen kao otvoren, zajednički prostor, open space se pretvorio u zatvorenost, u prostor gde se svako brani od drugih svojim slušalicama, ekranima i pogledom u pod.

Menadžment voli da kaže da zidovi prave barijere.
Istina je da barijere prave ljudi.
Ako ti je organizaciona kultura zatvorena, ako nema poverenja, ako ljudi ne osećaju potrebu da se diskutuju jedni sa drugima, skidanje zidova neće promeniti ništa osim troškova za molera.
I tu leži ironija: kada saradnja ne postoji, nije problem u rasporedu stolova, nego u onome kako vodiš firmu, i kakvu organizacionu kulturu živi ta kompanija. Ono što sam naučio kroz karijeru i kroz istraživanje u pripremi disertacije je da je organizaciona kultura kao voda. Ukoliko je ti ne oblikuješ, ne menadžuješ, sa stavom, “ma sve će to da se desi samo od sebe”, organizaciona kultura nadje svoj put, i svoj način da se uoblikuje. Često, ne u ono što menadžment kompanije želi.

Zašto se i dalje guramo u open space, zašto se to prodaje kao „rešenje za budućnost rada“, i zašto menadžment uporno brani koncept koji očigledno ne funkcioniše?
Možda zato što je lakše meriti kvadrate nego poverenje.
Ali pravo pitanje je: da li menadžment zaista veruje da ljudi rade bolje kada sede zajedno, ili je to samo mit koji niko ne sme da prizna da ne funkcioniše? Ja nisam smeo, priznajem.

posts.stimulus:
Japan je na drugoj planeti; mi čak ne možemo ni da kopiramo jer sebe smatramo previše pametnim. Cementiramo i uništavamo zelenilo

 JAPAN: PARKINGI SE TRANSFORMIŠU U VISEĆE BAŠTE 
Nije dovoljno biti efikasan. Morate biti zeleni, prozračni i poštovati životnu sredinu.

U Japanu, modernost se susreće sa prirodom na iznenađujući način: javni parkinzi su prekriveni cvetnim zelenim krovovima, stvarajući bujne prostore koji kombinuju funkcionalnost, estetiku i poštovanje životne sredine.

 Ove „viseće bašte“ nisu jednostavne dekoracije:
one su konkretan model urbane održivosti, koji se može ponoviti u našim gradovima!

 PREDNOSTI OVIH ZELENIH PARKING MESTA:

 Smanjenje gradske toplote – Biljke snižavaju temperature i suprotstavljaju se efektu toplotnog ostrva.

 Prirodna hladovina za automobile – Manje korišćenje klima uređaja i veća udobnost.

 Urbani biodiverzitet – Bezbedna utočišta za pčele, leptire i oprašivače, sve više ugrožene urbanizacijom.
 Bolji kvalitet vazduha – Biljke apsorbuju ugljen-dioksid, filtriraju prašinu i toksine.
 Estetska vrednost – Boje i mirisi koji pretvaraju parking u urbanu oazu.
 Psihološko blagostanje – Vizuelni kontakt sa prirodom, čak i na najneočekivanijim mestima, smanjuje stres i anksioznost.

 Inicijativa pod nazivom (plutajuće bašte) koja pokazuje kako inovacija može ići ruku pod ruku sa zaštitom životne sredine.
